### Post by DouglasAWh on 2008-11-30
So, I've read some posts here and on Fedora forums and things still aren't working.  Here's my grub as it stands

```

# grub.conf generated by anaconda
#
# Note that you do not have to rerun grub after making changes to this file
# NOTICE:  You have a /boot partition.  This means that
#          all kernel and initrd paths are relative to /boot/, eg.
#          root (hd0,2)
#          kernel /vmlinuz-version ro root=/dev/VolGroup00/LogVol00
#          initrd /initrd-version.img
#boot=/dev/sda
default=0
timeout=5
splashimage=(hd0,2)/grub/splash.xpm.gz
title Fedora (2.6.27.5-117.fc10.i686)
	root (hd0,2)
	kernel /vmlinuz-2.6.27.5-117.fc10.i686 ro root=UUID=b3a8eeb1-5933-40cb-980b-4b87c4357a2f rhgb quiet
	initrd /initrd-2.6.27.5-117.fc10.i686.img

#begin Ubuntu

title		Ubuntu 8.10, kernel 2.6.27-7-generic

kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.27-7-generic root=UUID=00114351-9bd0-4dab-8420-0d491c12e73f ro quiet splash 
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.27-7-generic
quiet

title		Ubuntu 8.10, kernel 2.6.27-7-generic (recovery mode)
root		(hd0,0)
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.27-7-generic root=UUID=00114351-9bd0-4dab-8420-0d491c12e73f ro  single
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.27-7-generic

title		Ubuntu 8.10, memtest86+
root		(hd0,0)
kernel		/boot/memtest86+.bin
quiet

### END DEBIAN AUTOMAGIC KERNELS LIST

```

Here's the output of fdisk -l

```

255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 12161 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 16065 * 512 = 8225280 bytes

Now, it looks to me like things are right if sda1 == hd0,0.  However, when I try to boot to Ubuntu, the splash screen loads and then it crashes hard.  Ubuntu was working fine before I installed.  Linux has really been frustrating me lately with wacom tablet, webcam and virtual machine issues, so please excuse my lack of patience for the situation. I've already put more effort into that than it's worth to me.

Thank you for your help.

### Post by caljohnsmith on 2008-11-30
Have you checked the UUID of sda1 to make sure it is correct for your Ubuntu entries? That might possibly be the problem. How about doing:
```
sudo blkid -c /dev/null | grep sda1
```
And make sure that UUID agrees with the one you are currently using, namely "00114351-9bd0-4dab-8420-0d491c12e73f". If that UUID is incorrect, you'll also have to modify your /etc/fstab in the Ubuntu partition. But let me know if that's the problem first, because it may not be. :)

---

### Post by caljohnsmith on 2008-11-30
Unfortunately, I think the subject you gave to your thread is looking like it might be accurate at this point. How about commenting out the "quiet splash" options from your Ubuntu entries in the menu.lst so you can see the boot messages, and hopefully get a better clue what is causing Ubuntu to crash:
```
title		Ubuntu 8.10, kernel 2.6.27-7-generic
root		(hd0,0)
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.27-7-generic root=UUID=00114351-9bd0-4dab-8420-0d491c12e73f ro [COLOR="Red"]#quiet splash[/COLOR] 
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.27-7-generic
quiet
```
It looks like you might have to back up your files and reinstall Ubuntu though, but maybe seeing what the boot messages are prior to crashing will help pinpoint the problem. Let me know how it goes.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2008-11-30
Have you tried to rebuild the initramfs:
```
sudo update-initramfs -u
```
You might have to chroot in if you are unable to get in through Ubuntu.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2008-11-30
> **DouglasAWh said:**
> ok, you lost me.  it doesn't sound like I do that from Fedora.  Do I do that from a LiveCD?  I've got one now.  Didn't before.  Thanks!

I am assuming that /dev/sda1 is Ubuntu.  If that is the case:
```
sudo mkdir /media/hardy
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/hardy
sudo mount -t proc none /media/hardy/proc
sudo mount -o bind /dev /media/hardy/dev
sudo chroot /media/hardy /bin/bash
```This should get you chrooted in.  The next step would be to do the following:
```
update-initramfs -u
```

I am thinking that you should be able to do that through Fedora, but the LiveCD should work also.

EDIT: I removed the sudo out of the update-initramfs -u command only because you are already root but it should not hurt anything if you used it with sudo.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2008-11-30
You might want to run fsck on /dev/sda1.  This [link]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4288766&postcount=4") says to use fsck -y /dev/sda1.  I have not run this before, so you might want to do a man fsck before trying just to see if you are comfortable about doing it.  You will need to unmount /dev/sda1 first:
```
sudo umount /media/ibex/proc
sudo umount /media/ibex
```

### Post by caljohnsmith on 2008-11-30
You could try using a different super block if one of the backup super blocks in the file system is valid; you can do that via these instructions:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5806996](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5806996)

Just use "sda1" instead of "sda5" in those instructions. Let me know if that works or not.

### Post by caljohnsmith on 2008-12-01
> **DouglasAWh said:**
> Looked at the instructions and 32768 was indeed the first superblock.  No error on mounting that.  But, when I did e2fsck I got superblock errors again.  Should I just try another superblock even though I didn't get errors mounting?
So did you first unmount sda1 and then do:
```
sudo e2fsck -b 32768 /dev/sda1
```
And that still returns superblock errors? If so, it shouldn't hurt to try other superblocks and see if they might work.

---

### Post by geirha on 2008-12-04
You can only have four partitions on a harddrive. Four partitions turned out to be a bit few, so you can get more partitions by setting one of the four partitions as a special extended partition. The extended partition can contain alot of (logical) partitions, making the total number of partitions you can have much higher than 4. So, in your case it doesn't make sense to list it that extended partition along with the "regular" partitions.

The four primary partitions you can have will get numbered 1-4 by linux. E.g. /dev/sda1, /dev/sda2, /dev/sda3, /dev/sda4. The extended partition must be one of those four, and any logical partitions you put on the extended partititon will be numbered from 5 and up (no matter how many primary partitions you actually have).

---

### Post by DouglasAWh on 2008-12-04
> **geirha said:**
> 
The four primary partitions you can have will get numbered 1-4 by linux. E.g. /dev/sda1, /dev/sda2, /dev/sda3, /dev/sda4. The extended partition must be one of those four, and any logical partitions you put on the extended partititon will be numbered from 5 and up (no matter how many primary partitions you actually have).

ok, so the swap on /dev/sda5 isn't a problem?

---

### Post by geirha on 2008-12-04
> **DouglasAWh said:**
> ok, so the swap on /dev/sda5 isn't a problem?

No problem at all. Linux does not care whether its filesystems are on primary or logical partitions.
